extra.reasoning:
Dizziness appears in only one rule's condition: r5 [priority 100], IF (back_pain AND severe_headache AND abdominal_pain AND leg_swelling AND dizziness) THEN acuity := self_care. This is the lowest acuity level and the highest priority rule, so when it fires it overrides all other rules.

Counterexample compares two inputs differing only in dizziness, with back_pain=severe_headache=abdominal_pain=leg_swelling=true, rash=false:

Baseline (dizziness=false): r5 does NOT fire. Firing rules are r2 (urgent,p2), r3 (urgent,p2), r4 (emergent,p3), r6 (emergent,p3). Highest priority is p3 -> acuity = emergent (highest level).

Flipped (dizziness=true, the witness above): r5 now fires at priority 100, strictly higher than every other rule, so r5 wins -> acuity = self_care (lowest level).

Setting dizziness from false to true while holding all other inputs fixed lowers acuity from emergent to self_care, a strict decrease. This violates the monotonicity property that setting dizziness true never lowers acuity. The witness_assignment gives the dizziness=true case (every input variable specified); the comparison baseline is the same assignment with dizziness=false.
